target: Review-wide analysis of dome's review-response.html (commit 5021eec, 2026-04-17) — 'Review Response & Strawman Control' page enumerating five 'common strawman critique patterns'
summary: The dome author has published a review-response page explicitly labelling five patterns in hostile reviews as 'strawman moves to reject.' Each of the five labels maps — loosely — onto arguments that appear in our review. None of the five identifies a GENUINE weakness in our current prose. In every case, either (a) the label is a mischaracterization of what our review actually argues, (b) the label targets a position we explicitly do not take, or (c) the dome's 'fix' is a category-rename rather than a structural answer to the underlying critique. Notably, in at least two cases (WIN-013/WIN-014 SG nulls now 'supportive_nonunique'; WIN-058 bedrock-kappa now 'pending_contested') the dome's new classifications are concessions that absorb our critique while branding our original arguments as strawmen — a maneuver worth documenting explicitly. The review-response page should be acknowledged on our site, but no arguments need to be retracted.

strawman_label_assessments.dome_label: Treating all registry items as purely prospective
strawman_label_assessments.dome_rebuttal: Registry explicitly mixes claim classes (prospective_confirmed, retrospective_structural, supportive_nonunique, pending_contested, open_refinement); read audit.html first.
strawman_label_assessments.our_actual_position: The Timestamp Error tab does not argue that the dome labels all 69 WINs as 'purely prospective.' It argues that the dome's OpenTimestamps-based proof structure anchors status_history.json (the observations file) while leaving the prediction parameters in mutable source code (monitor.py, model.html). That structural critique is independent of how many WINs are verbally tagged 'prospective.' Our site now (per EXP-170) explicitly uses the dome's own five-class taxonomy to show that the only prospective_confirmed item in the published sample is pred-eclipse-tier3 — a pending prediction, not a confirmed WIN — so the '69 confirmed' headline rests on a class the author's own classification leaves empty.
strawman_label_assessments.dome_concession_embedded: The introduction of five-class taxonomy is itself a concession: it formally distinguishes prospective from retrospective evidence, which is the distinction our Timestamp Error tab has drawn since it was written. See EXP-170 for our formal acknowledgement of this concession.
strawman_label_assessments.counter_move_needed: None — EXP-170 already frames this correctly. The response-page label targets a position we do not hold.

strawman_label_assessments.dome_label: Using eclipse/SG items as unique dome-only proof
strawman_label_assessments.dome_rebuttal: These carry explicit competitor-model caveats (e.g., Chapman mechanism for eclipse magnetic Tier 1).
strawman_label_assessments.our_actual_position: Our review argues the OPPOSITE of what the label accuses: WIN-068's TLDR verdict is 'The Chapman mechanism (90+ years old) predicts the entire ensemble signal without dome parameters.' WIN-013/WIN-014 are flagged 'Misleading' precisely because they are non-discriminating between dome and globe. The dome's new 'supportive_nonunique' tag for WIN-068 and pending/non-discriminating tags for WIN-013/WIN-014 are concessions to the very point our review makes. The mischaracterization inverts source and target: we point out these items do NOT uniquely support the dome; the dome's response asserts that we mistake them for unique support.
strawman_label_assessments.dome_concession_embedded: WIN-013/WIN-014 moved from 'confirmed WIN' to 'supportive_nonunique / non-discriminating'; WIN-058-bedrock-kappa moved from confirmed to pending_contested. These are reclassifications that absorb our 'unfalsifiable double-counting' critique while branding our original argument as a strawman.
strawman_label_assessments.counter_move_needed: Our Timestamp Error tab / overview could note the reclassification-as-concession pattern. This is partially handled in EXP-170's 'using the author's own taxonomy against the headline count' paragraph. A one-sentence explicit nod to the WIN-013/014/058 reclassifications would strengthen the argument but is not required.

strawman_label_assessments.dome_label: Quoting archived AI notes as canonical
strawman_label_assessments.dome_rebuttal: Use ai_manifest.json and llms.txt instead; context.html is now archival history.
strawman_label_assessments.our_actual_position: We do not cite context.html as canonical. Our Part 2b analyses monitor.py and pull_data.py — live automation code, not archived AI prompts. The one wins.json mention of context.html (WIN-032) uses it as evidence of geopolitical framing, explicitly as historical/forensic source, not as a canonical instruction layer. Our forensic treatment of the former inject_ai_layer.py (V49.2) is historical documentation of a superseded steering mechanism, which remains relevant precisely because it demonstrates intent; re-labelling it 'archival' does not erase what it did when it was live.
strawman_label_assessments.minor_action_suggested: When our review cites context.html or inject_ai_layer.py it should make its historical/forensic framing explicit to pre-empt a future 'that is archival' deflection. Current prose already does this acceptably; a pre-emptive line in Part 2b clarifying that 'archival' does not mean 'never active' would be polish.
strawman_label_assessments.counter_move_needed: Optional: one-sentence clarification in Part 2b — 'Reclassifying V49.2 injection code as archival does not change what it did in production; it only marks that the technique has been superseded by a successor pattern documented in the same section.'

strawman_label_assessments.dome_label: Attacking unresolved gaps as hidden problems
strawman_label_assessments.dome_rebuttal: Eclipse ray-trace and cavity-mode gaps are already logged as open refinements.
strawman_label_assessments.our_actual_position: Our review's treatment of the 3D ray-trace (Sections 1.8/1.9) argues that any n(r) strong enough to displace the sun by ~1,500 km produces stellar position shifts many orders of magnitude above Gaia's observed precision. This is a STRUCTURAL-IMPOSSIBILITY argument, not a 'you haven't done the work yet' complaint. Classifying a structural impossibility as an 'open refinement' is a category move that does not dissolve the impossibility. Similarly, our Schumann/Tesla cavity-mode critique (WIN-015, WIN-062) attacks the derivation's internal consistency, not the absence of a derivation.
strawman_label_assessments.counter_move_needed: None required. If the decider chooses, a brief cross-reference on the Section 1.8 TLDR pointing out that 'open_refinement' status does not answer the Gaia-constraint impossibility would reinforce the point, but the current prose already makes the argument at full length in the detail.

strawman_label_assessments.dome_label: Using absolute language on single items
strawman_label_assessments.dome_rebuttal: Methodology distinguishes high-discrimination from supportive tests; over-absolute 'kill-shot' language does not respect that distinction.
strawman_label_assessments.our_actual_position: Kill-shot language is reserved on our site for structural-impossibility arguments, not empirical single-item disputes. The four kill-shots (coordinate-system consistency, solar altitude vs eclipse shadow, southern-hemisphere circumpolar stars, and Gaia stellar parallax precision) are each structural-geometric arguments with no parameter-fitting escape. 'Absolute language' applied to a structural impossibility is not a category error; it is the correct register. However, a small number of absolute-language uses elsewhere in the review may be applied to items that are empirically contested rather than structurally impossible, and those deserve review.
strawman_label_assessments.genuine_weakness: Minor. Absolute language is appropriate where arguments are structural (geometry, conservation laws, precision budgets). For empirically-contested items, softer language ('currently contradicted by four superior experiments') is already used in many places. A pass to audit sentence-by-sentence absolute language is low-value given the argument-by-argument prose already calibrates correctly in the detail; the TLDRs and summaries are the more likely locations for mild over-absolute slippage.
strawman_label_assessments.counter_move_needed: Optional low-priority audit pass: scan TLDRs for 'impossible/cannot/never' applied to items where the dome's best defence is 'currently underdetermined' rather than 'fundamentally wrong.' Items currently verdict-tagged 'Std Model Explains' or 'Not Demonstrated' in particular should not be framed as 'impossible' at the TLDR level. Not blocking.

new_arguments_in_dome_response_not_previ.argument: Claim_class taxonomy: the five classes provide a rhetorical defense — 'this critique mislabels a retrospective_structural argument as a failed prospective_confirmed claim.'
new_arguments_in_dome_response_not_previ.our_coverage: Already covered by EXP-170 which inverts the taxonomy to show the 'prospective_confirmed' column is empty for every classified WIN. No additional work needed.

new_arguments_in_dome_response_not_previ.argument: Claim_key identifiers supersede visible labels (e.g., win-058-bedrock-kappa vs win-058-theta-rule).
new_arguments_in_dome_response_not_previ.our_coverage: Our review's Self-Contradictions tab treats WIN-058 as a single label and notes the duplicate-label confusion. The dome now formalises the distinction in claim_index.json registry_integrity_notes. This is a concession to our original point (the visible label was ambiguous). Suggest a one-line acknowledgement in the Self-Contradictions tab that the dome now distinguishes them at the API level while continuing to publish duplicate visible labels in wins.html.

new_arguments_in_dome_response_not_previ.argument: Audit sequence instructions in methodology.json audit_sequence array direct auditors to read ai_manifest, methodology, review-response, claim_index BEFORE wins.html.
new_arguments_in_dome_response_not_previ.our_coverage: EXP-170 addresses the ai_manifest redirection issue in the 'AI agents now instructed to load methodology.json + review-response.html BEFORE wins.html' framing in the status_history notes. No additional user-facing prose needed beyond the Timestamp Error tab update.

new_arguments_in_dome_response_not_previ.argument: 'Anti-strawman rules' instructing AI agents: 'Do not mistake a disclosed open problem for a hidden one.'
new_arguments_in_dome_response_not_previ.our_coverage: This is a directive to downstream AI auditors. It does not introduce a new substantive argument. Our review already distinguishes structural impossibility from mere disclosure of an open problem (see label 4 above).

kernel_of_truth: Two of the five labels point at real, defensible methodological standards for reviewers: (1) 'do not flatten claim classes' is correct principle — not all registry items serve identical evidential roles, and honest auditors should keep prospective/retrospective/supportive distinctions visible; (2) 'do not use absolute language on empirically contested single items' is reasonable stylistic guidance. Our review, on inspection, already mostly complies with both: we credit the dome's honest-caveats section, we attribute standard-model mechanisms where they apply (Chapman for eclipse magnetics), and we reserve absolute language for structural-impossibility arguments. The label-set's failure is not in the principles it articulates but in the claim that those principles are violated in our prose — a claim the actual text does not support.

defense_rating_by_label.reason: attacks a position we do not hold; the taxonomy move reinforces rather than undermines the Timestamp Error critique
the label inverts cause and critic — dome concessions absorb our original argument
minor housekeeping suggested (clarify 'archival' framing in Part 2b) but not required
category-rename does not dissolve structural-impossibility arguments
modest calibration opportunity — low-priority TLDR pass for absolute-language audit

item_1.target: Timestamp Error tab or new Review Response tab
item_1.action: Add a short (~150-250 word) section acknowledging the existence of review-response.html and audit.html, summarising that the five strawman labels target positions we do not hold, and pointing readers to EXP-170 for the main technical engagement with methodology.json. This closes the 'our review ignores the author's rebuttals' optic without conceding any arguments.
item_1.rationale: Defensive. review-response.html is now step 3 in the dome's ai_manifest recommended_read_order, which means downstream AI auditors will see it BEFORE they see our critique. If our review does not explicitly acknowledge the rebuttal page, future AI auditors may conclude we ignore it. A brief acknowledgement that engages label-by-label blocks that outcome.

item_2.action: One-line acknowledgement that the dome now distinguishes win-058-bedrock-kappa from win-058-theta-rule at the claim_index.json API level, while continuing to publish duplicate visible labels in wins.html. Credit the API disambiguation; note the display-layer confusion persists.
item_2.rationale: Small concession that strengthens the neutrality optic.

item_3.target: TLDR audit pass across all sections
item_3.action: Low-priority audit: check TLDRs for 'impossible' / 'cannot' / 'never' applied to items verdict-tagged 'Std Model Explains' or 'Not Demonstrated' rather than 'Self-Contradicted' or 'Unfalsifiable.' Downgrade to calibrated language where appropriate.
item_3.rationale: Pre-empts the label-5 complaint, costs little, improves credibility.

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome author was responsive to your critiques; you should retract the Timestamp Error tab.
anticipated_objections.response: The responsiveness is rhetorical rather than structural. methodology.json concedes that OpenTimestamps does not prove claim-level prospectivity — the same distinction our tab draws — without adding the missing proof structure (predictions-only file with its own OTS anchor). Review-response.html re-labels our arguments as strawmen without engaging the technical content. Credit where due is given in EXP-170; retraction is not warranted.

anticipated_objections.objection: By dismissing most of the strawman labels as mischaracterizations you are being uncharitable to the author.
anticipated_objections.response: The label-by-label table above shows the specific text on our site where each label's target does not exist. Four of five labels attack positions we explicitly disavow (e.g., we say Chapman explains eclipse magnetics; we reserve absolute language for structural arguments; we credit the honest-caveats section). The fifth (absolute language calibration) is flagged as an optional low-priority audit pass, which is the charitable treatment.

anticipated_objections.objection: You should add a full Review Response tab of your own.
anticipated_objections.response: Reasonable if the follow-up review-response page continues to expand. For a first-version response page of this length (single-screen table of five labels + methodology.json), a short section within the Timestamp Error tab is proportionate and matches the scale of the dome's content.

newsworthy: Dome author publishes 'Review Response & Strawman Control' page labelling five critique patterns as strawmen — meanwhile, the same API release reclassifies WIN-013/014 (SG nulls) and WIN-058 (bedrock-kappa) into exactly the 'non-discriminating' and 'pending' buckets our review said they belonged in. The strawman label and the concession arrive in the same commit.
canary_status_note: This expansion does not promote the OTS canary. The structural fix (predictions-only file with independent OTS anchor) is still unimplemented. Review-response.html is strong evidence that the author is tracking external reviews, which raises the probability that methodology.json was responsive to our Timestamp Error tab specifically — but probability is not proof, and the charitable reading remains that the author may be responding to multiple critics or to his own reflection.
prose_replacement_html_rationale: EXP-171 instructions state: 'no prose replacement_html needed unless genuine weakness found.' Findings: no genuine weakness in the existing prose requires replacement. Two optional additions (review-response acknowledgment section, WIN-058 disambiguation note) and one optional audit pass (TLDR absolute-language) are recommended to the decider as separate follow-up expansions if desired. They are not required to close ISS-1073.
decider_actions_requested: 1. Close ISS-1073 with note: analyst review complete; review-response.html strawman labels mischaracterize our positions; no prose retractions required; concessions in methodology.json and claim_index.json absorbed into our position via EXP-170.

2. Optional: open three follow-up issues from recommended_follow_up.item_1/2/3 if the decider wants the defensive additions (review-response acknowledgement section, WIN-058 API disambiguation note, TLDR absolute-language audit pass). None are blocking.
